title: Samsung Reportedly Restarts 1.4nm Push, Targets 2029 Mass Production to Close Gap with TSMC, Intel
gist: Samsung has resumed commercializing its 1.4nm (SF1.4) foundry process with 2029 mass production, redirecting focus to 2nm yield stabilization; the pivot already moved its Exynos 2800 to an advanced 2nm node, while TSMC eyes 2H28 mass production without High-NA EUV and Intel targets 14A high-volume in 2029.

insights.claim: Samsung has resumed efforts to commercialize its 1.4nm (SF1.4) foundry process with mass production now slated for 2029, per The Bell, placing it slightly behind Intel and TSMC.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: Confirms Samsung is back in the leading-edge race but on a later timeline, framing a multi-year competitive gap to monitor.
insights.locator: According to The Bell, the company has resumed efforts

insights.claim: Samsung pushed its 1.4nm target from 2027 to 2029 to strengthen its 2nm (SF2) and derivative SF2P processes, signaling a strategic shift toward yield stabilization and process optimization over aggressive node advancement.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: A yield-first strategy suggests Samsung is prioritizing winning 2nm business now over racing to the bleeding edge, changing how to judge its foundry momentum.
insights.quote: signaling a shift toward yield stabilization and process optimization over aggressive node advancement

insights.claim: The strategic pivot is visible at product level: Samsung shifted its next flagship smartphone processor, the Exynos 2800, from the planned 1.4nm node to an advanced version of its 2nm process.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: Moving a flagship SoC off 1.4nm concretely confirms the delay is real and 2nm is Samsung's near-term workhorse node.
insights.locator: A previous Global Economic News report suggested

insights.claim: Samsung has shared its 1.4nm process roadmap with major equipment makers including Applied Materials and Lam Research and requested early development of process equipment from domestic and overseas partners.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: Roadmap-sharing with tool vendors is an early, tangible signal of 1.4nm intent and future equipment order flow.
insights.locator: Samsung is also understood to have recently requested

insights.claim: Samsung has already installed ASML's next-generation High-NA EUV at its NRD-K facility, to be applied to select layers starting from the 1.4nm process.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: Installed High-NA tooling is Samsung's key technical differentiator entering the 1.4nm race.
insights.locator: Samsung Electronics has already installed ASML's next-generation High-NA

insights.claim: TSMC is targeting 1.4nm pilot production as early as 3Q27 with mass production in 2H28, per Economic Daily News.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: Sets the benchmark TSMC timeline Samsung must close against; 2H28 is the date to watch for leading-edge leadership.
insights.locator: TSMC is targeting 1.4nm pilot production as early as 3Q27

insights.claim: TSMC is expected to skip ASML's High-NA EUV through 2029, meaning its 1.4nm node would proceed without the advanced system adopted by Intel and Samsung, per Tom's Hardware.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: TSMC betting it can lead at 1.4nm without High-NA is a divergent strategy; if it works it reframes the ROI case for expensive High-NA tools.
insights.locator: Tom's Hardware notes that TSMC is expected to skip ASML's High-NA EUV

insights.claim: Intel's 18A-P process has entered pilot production, with 14A risk production reportedly targeted for 2028 and high-volume manufacturing in 2029, per Reuters.
insights.speaker: Xebec
insights.so_what: Puts Intel's 14A on roughly the same 2029 high-volume horizon as Samsung, making them direct 1.4nm timeline rivals.
insights.locator: For Intel, Reuters reports that its 18A-P process has already entered pilot production
